reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Computability

map.condition: Language accepted by TM that always halts
Language accepted by TM that may loop on non-members
Checking if a problem is undecidable via reduction

formulas.name: Chomsky Hierarchy Containment
formulas.plain: Regular ⊂ CFL ⊂ Recursive ⊂ RE ⊂ All Languages
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing expressive power of language classes

terms.means: Type 3 — recognized by finite automata
Type 2 — recognized by pushdown automata
Type 1 — decided by halting TM
Type 0 — recognized by any TM

formulas.trap: RE is NOT the same as Recursive; confusing the two causes wrong answers about decidability

formulas.name: Grammar Types vs Language Classes
formulas.plain: Type 0 (Unrestricted grammar) <-> RE; Type 1 (Context-Sensitive Grammar) <-> CSL subset Recursive
formulas.whenToUse: Mapping grammar types to their corresponding language classes

terms.means: Unrestricted grammar — no restrictions on productions
Context-sensitive grammar — productions are length non-decreasing
Context-Sensitive Language — recognized by linear bounded automaton

formulas.trap: Type 0 generates RE languages, NOT all recursive languages; recursive is a strict subset of RE

formulas.name: Recursive Language Definition
formulas.latex: L \text{ is Recursive} \iff \exists\, \text{TM } M : M \text{ halts on every input, accepts } L
formulas.plain: L is Recursive iff there exists a TM M that halts on every input and accepts exactly L
formulas.whenToUse: Determining whether a language is decidable (always-halting TM exists)

terms.means: Turing machine that is a decider (halts on all inputs)
The language being decided

formulas.trap: Recursive does NOT mean the language is finite; all regular and CFL are recursive but may be infinite

formulas.name: Closure Properties of Recursive Languages
formulas.plain: Recursive languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star, homomorphism
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a constructed language is recursive using closure properties

terms.means: Complement of language L
Concatenation of two languages
Kleene star (zero or more concatenations)

formulas.trap: Recursive is closed under complement — this distinguishes it from RE (which is not)

formulas.name: Lexicographic Enumeration ↔ Recursive
formulas.latex: L \text{ enumerable in lexicographic order} \iff L \text{ is Recursive}
formulas.plain: L can be enumerated in lexicographic order iff L is Recursive
formulas.whenToUse: When a question says strings can be listed in alphabetic/lexicographic order

terms.means: Strings sorted by dictionary order (length and alphabetical)

formulas.trap: Enumerable in ANY order implies RE; enumerable in LEXICOGRAPHIC order implies Recursive — the order matters

formulas.name: Complement of Recursive Language
formulas.plain: If L is recursive then complement(L) is also recursive
formulas.whenToUse: Proving complement of a decidable language is decidable

terms.means: Complement — all strings NOT in L over the same alphabet

formulas.trap: The TM for complement simply swaps accept and reject states — this only works when the TM always halts

formulas.name: Recursively Enumerable Language Definition
formulas.latex: L \text{ is RE} \iff \exists\, \text{TM } M : M \text{ accepts } w \iff w \in L\ (\text{may loop on } w \notin L)
formulas.plain: L is RE iff there exists a TM M that accepts w if w in L, and loops or rejects if w not in L
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying languages recognizable by some Turing machine

terms.means: Recognizer TM — not required to halt on all inputs
TM runs forever without accepting or rejecting

formulas.trap: RE machines may loop forever on non-members — they do NOT have to reject; only Recursive requires halting

formulas.name: Closure Properties of RE Languages
formulas.plain: RE is closed under union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star; RE is NOT closed under complement
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing operations on RE languages to determine if the result is still RE

terms.means: Complement of L — may not be RE if L is RE

formulas.trap: L in RE and complement(L) in RE together imply L is Recursive — a frequent GATE trick question

formulas.name: RE ∩ co-RE = Recursive
formulas.plain: L is in RE and complement(L) is in RE if and only if L is Recursive
formulas.whenToUse: When both a language and its complement are recognized by TMs — conclude the language is decidable

terms.means: Class of languages whose complement is RE

formulas.trap: If L is RE but NOT recursive, then complement(L) cannot be RE — this is a biconditional, not one-way

formulas.name: Enumeration Characterization of RE
formulas.latex: L \text{ is RE} \iff \exists \text{ enumerator TM that prints all strings of } L
formulas.plain: L is RE iff there is a TM enumerator that prints every string of L (in any order, possibly with repetitions)
formulas.whenToUse: Connecting the concept of effective enumeration to RE languages

terms.means: TM with printer that outputs language strings one by one

formulas.trap: Enumeration in arbitrary order implies RE; only lexicographic order implies Recursive — do not confuse

formulas.name: Halting Problem / ATM Undecidability
formulas.latex: A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\} \text{ is RE but NOT Recursive}
formulas.plain: ATM = {<M,w> | M accepts w} is RE but not Recursive (undecidable)
formulas.whenToUse: Establishing the base undecidable language for reductions

terms.means: Acceptance problem for TMs — the canonical undecidable language
Encoding of TM M and input string w

formulas.trap: ATM is NOT in Recursive (undecidable) but IS in RE — it has a recognizer but no decider

formulas.name: Many-One Reduction (Mapping Reduction)
formulas.plain: A <=_m B means: if B is decidable then A is decidable; if A is undecidable then B is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: Proving undecidability by reducing from a known undecidable language

terms.means: A is many-one reducible to B via a computable total function
Computable function such that w in A iff f(w) in B

formulas.trap: Reduction direction matters: to prove B is undecidable, reduce FROM a known undecidable A TO B (not the other way)

formulas.name: Rice's Theorem
formulas.latex: \forall \text{ non-trivial property } P \text{ of RE languages: } L_P = \{\langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \in P\} \text{ is undecidable}
formulas.plain: Every non-trivial semantic property of TM-recognized languages is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: Instantly classifying questions asking about properties of what a TM accepts

terms.means: Non-trivial property — satisfied by some RE languages and not others
The language recognized by TM M
Encoding of Turing machine M

formulas.trap: Rice's theorem applies only to SEMANTIC properties (about the language) not SYNTACTIC ones (about TM structure); also only to non-trivial properties

formulas.name: Halting Problem Undecidability
formulas.latex: HALT_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\} \text{ is undecidable (RE, not Recursive)}
formulas.plain: HALT_TM = {<M,w> | M halts on w} is undecidable — no TM can decide it
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a TM halts on a specific input — always undecidable

terms.means: Halting problem — does TM M halt on input w?

formulas.trap: The halting problem is undecidable even if we only ask 'does it halt on empty input' — all variants are undecidable

formulas.name: Trivially Decidable/Known Language
formulas.latex: L = \Sigma^* \text{ or } L = \emptyset \Rightarrow L \text{ is Recursive (trivially decidable)}
formulas.plain: L = Sigma* (all strings) or L = {} (empty) implies L is Recursive
formulas.whenToUse: When a language is either all strings or no strings — it is always recursive

terms.means: Set of all strings over alphabet Sigma
Empty language — no strings

formulas.trap: The GATE trick: 'L = {0} if P != NP, else Sigma*' — since either case yields a fixed known regular language, L is always recursive regardless of whether P=NP is resolved

formulas.name: Union of RE and co-RE may not be Recursive
formulas.plain: Union of an RE language and a co-RE language need not be Recursive
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing combinations of RE and co-RE languages

terms.means: Languages whose complement is in RE

formulas.trap: Do not assume that combining RE and co-RE languages always yields a recursive language; check each case separately

formulas.name: Subset / Superset Decidability
formulas.plain: A subset of a recursive language is not necessarily recursive
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether a subset or superset of a decidable language is decidable

terms.means: L is a subset of L'

formulas.trap: Recursiveness is NOT closed downward under subset — any RE language is a subset of Sigma* (which is recursive) yet may be undecidable

formulas.name: RE ∩ RE = RE; co-RE ∩ co-RE = co-RE
formulas.plain: RE is closed under intersection; co-RE is closed under intersection
formulas.whenToUse: Computing intersections or unions of RE / co-RE languages

terms.means: Class of complements of RE languages

formulas.trap: RE is closed under union AND intersection but NOT complement; co-RE is the mirror image
